coredata.dc:title: Revolutionizing dye-sensitized solar cells with nanomaterials for enhanced photoelectric performance

coredata.dc:description: Dye-sensitized solar cells have emerged as a promising alternative to conventional solar cells due to their cost-effectiveness and ease of fabrication. In recent years, the integration of nanomaterials into dye-sensitized solar cells has garnered substantial attention, offering new avenues to enhance their photoelectric performance. This review comprehensively examines the role of nanomaterials in elevating the efficiency and functionality of dye-sensitized solar cells. The fundamental principles of dye-sensitized solar cells are introduced, emphasizing the intricate interplay between crucial components that enable light absorption, charge separation, and electron transport. Nanomaterials play important roles across the entire spectrum of photoelectric conversion of dye-sensitized solar cells, e.g., constructing light-trapping architectures, creating interlayer transmission bridges, and facilitating charge carrier transport pathways, thus offering cost-effective alternatives to precious metals. Furthermore, this review concludes the central role of nanomaterials in shaping the landscape of flexible optoelectronic materials, highlighting their paramount importance in this area. The extensive scientific insights presented herein not only showcase the cutting-edge achievements in solar energy conversion efficiency but also serve as a comprehensive guide for the proper selection of nanomaterials. Finally, this work outlines the forthcoming challenges and offers insights into promising research avenues based on a comprehensive examination of various scholarly pursuits. Unlike other reviews that focus on the analyses of structures or components, this review concentrates on the impact of nanomaterials on the overall photoelectric performance of dye-sensitized solar cells. By fostering cross-disciplinary collaboration and advancing relevant research, dye-sensitized solar cells hold promise as a significant contributor to the global transition toward clean and renewable energy sources.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dye-sensitized solar cells have emerged as a promising alternative to conventional solar cells due to their cost-effectiveness and ease of fabrication. In recent years, the integration of nanomaterials into dye-sensitized solar cells has garnered substantial attention, offering new avenues to enhance their photoelectric performance. This review comprehensively examines the role of nanomaterials in elevating the efficiency and functionality of dye-sensitized solar cells. The fundamental principles of dye-sensitized solar cells are introduced, emphasizing the intricate interplay between crucial components that enable light absorption, charge separation, and electron transport. Nanomaterials play important roles across the entire spectrum of photoelectric conversion of dye-sensitized solar cells, e.g., constructing light-trapping architectures, creating interlayer transmission bridges, and facilitating charge carrier transport pathways, thus offering cost-effective alternatives to precious metals. Furthermore, this review concludes the central role of nanomaterials in shaping the landscape of flexible optoelectronic materials, highlighting their paramount importance in this area. The extensive scientific insights presented herein not only showcase the cutting-edge achievements in solar energy conversion efficiency but also serve as a comprehensive guide for the proper selection of nanomaterials. Finally, this work outlines the forthcoming challenges and offers insights into promising research avenues based on a comprehensive examination of various scholarly pursuits. Unlike other reviews that focus on the analyses of structures or components, this review concentrates on the impact of nanomaterials on the overall photoelectric performance of dye-sensitized solar cells. By fostering cross-disciplinary collaboration and advancing relevant research, dye-sensitized solar cells hold promise as a significant contributor to the global transition toward clean and renewable energy sources.

ce:section.ce:para: The pursuit of a low-carbon economy has become the prevailing path for sustainable development across the globe. Numerous nations and regions have rallied behind ambitious climate targets, marked by the objectives of achieving “zero carbon” and “carbon neutrality”. Establishing an innovative power ecosystem centered around renewable energy sources is of paramount importance. Solar energy stands out as a particularly promising resource, characterized by its abundant availability and environmentally friendly nature. Its pivotal role in driving carbon-neutral initiatives cannot be understated. A projection of China's energy landscape for 2060 illustrates the dominance of solar energy, commanding a substantial 47% share (Global Energy Interconnection Development and Cooperation Organization, 2021). Solar energy harnessing is achieved via three major pathways: photoelectric conversion, photothermal conversion, and photochemical conversion, among which solar photovoltaics have emerged as an indispensable solution for generating renewable electricity. Through the photoelectric conversion process, solar radiation is directly transformed into electrical energy via the photovoltaic effect.

Solar cells are responsible for determining the efficiency of solar energy utilization and the potential for electricity generation. Solar cells are categorized into four distinct generations based on their development trajectory. Crystalline silicon solar cells hold prominence within the solar photovoltaics industry, boasting remarkable conversion efficiency and stability. The first-generation solar cells have witnessed the utilization of absorbent layers such as monocrystalline silicon, polysilicon, and amorphous silicon, in which monocrystalline silicon solar cells have achieved the pinnacle of lab conversion efficiency at 27.6% (Kashyap et al., 2022). Polysilicon solar cells emerge as a cost-effective alternative to their monocrystalline silicon counterparts, substantially reducing costs while maintaining a considerable degree of efficiency.

In the second-generation solar cells, copper indium gallium selenium (CIGS) (Zhang et al., 2021) and cadmium telluride (CdTe) (Al-Kuhaili, 2021) materials stand out as their exemplary representatives. These thin-film solar cells, known for their streamlined fabrication processes, rely on fundamental constituents such as cadmium telluride (Al-Kuhaili, 2021; Uličná et al., 2017), gallium arsenide (Mangla and Roy, 2020), and copper indium gallium selenide (Farooq et al., 2020; Nwakanma et al., 2021; Yusoff, 2022). In addition, the efficiency of these solar cells tends to decrease over time due to inherent stability issues. In the face of pressing energy challenges characterized by rampant consumption and environmental pollution, the pursuit of the next-generation photoelectric conversion focuses on harnessing solar energy in a green, efficient, economical, and enduring way.

Third-generation solar cells offer a cost-effective approach to electricity generation, seamlessly integrating multiple energy thresholds, cost-effective preparation methods, and environmentally friendly raw materials. In 2003, Green's research group predicted photoelectric efficiency and power generation costs for the three generations of solar cells, as shown in Fig. 1 . The power generation costs are 1.00–3.50 US$/W and 0.10–0.50 US$/W for first- and third-generation photovoltaic technologies, respectively (Conibeer, 2007). Although the predicted values exceed actual data (as of March 2023 to November 2023, power generation costs from 0.259 US$/W to 0.147 US$/W for the 210 mm bifacial mono PERC module (Li et al., 2020)), the trend is consistent, i.e., third-generation power generation costs are lower than those of first-generation. Beyond cost considerations, these emerging solar cells also break the efficiency constraints of first-generation solar cells imposed by the single bandgap limitation (Conibeer, 2007). The key players in emerging solar cells are dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), organic solar cells, and perovskite solar cells. While some studies categorize these emerging devices as third-generation solar cells, others reserve this classification for tandem/multi-junction and other concepts capable of surpassing the 33% single-junction efficiency limit. Collectively, they all have promoted advancements in the photoelectric industry, improving both manufacturing costs and photoelectric efficiency. Discussions are already underway for fourth-generation solar cells, which explore a wide array of manufacturing materials, including organic, inorganic, and hybrid materials. The primary objective in fourth-generation solar cell design is to combine high efficiency with exceptional stability. Although the relevant technologies are still under active research and development, they have already demonstrated great potential.

DSSCs are considered as a viable and economical alternative to the first-generation and second-generation solar cells and have attracted substantial attention. Traditional DSSCs consist of a mesoporous semiconductor film sensitized with light-absorbing dye molecules, bridging the gap between organic and inorganic materials to facilitate efficient photo-to-electron conversion. The distinctive architecture of DSSCs operates under a wide range of light conditions, including diffuse and low-intensity sunlight, making them an attractive option for indoor and outdoor applications. When DSSCs have showcased remarkable progress, challenges remain to be addressed to unlock their potential fully. One of the critical challenges pertains to achieving higher energy conversion efficiencies and long-term stability, especially in real-world applications. Innovative approaches are necessary that can manipulate the intricate interplay between light absorption, charge transfer, and electron transport processes within the DSSC architecture.

The integration of nanomaterials into DSSCs has emerged as a transformative strategy to enhance their photoelectric performance further. Nanomaterials, with their unique size-dependent properties and tunable characteristics, offer a platform for precisely engineering DSSC components. This synergy between nanotechnology and photovoltaics has unlocked novel avenues for improving light absorption, charge separation, electron transport, and overall device efficiency. To advance the entire solar cell industry, applying nanomaterials seeks a balance between cost-effectiveness and high efficiency. Nanomaterial/nanotechnology-based DSSCs provide a solution for countries and regions abundant in solar energy sources to establish conventional solar power plants especially in rugged terrain with economic constraints. Compared with the rigid structure of silicon-based solar cells, the flexibility of DSSCs promotes the integration of photoelectric technology into daily life, including applications in transportation equipment and functional fabrics. Sustainability assessments, such as analysis of costs and monitoring of emissions, will be further developed into small units with the development of nanomaterial/nanotechnology-enhanced DSSCs.

This review provides a comprehensive overview of the advancements and insights gained from the incorporation of nanomaterials in DSSCs to improve their photoelectric performance, and to contribute to an in-depth understanding of how nanomaterials are reshaping the landscape of DSSCs technology. Recent review articles have provided insights into the latest improvements and advanced strategies in DSSCs (Kumavat et al., 2017; Gong et al., 2017; Khan et al., 2017; Ahmad et al., 2017). For example, Kumavat et al. (2017) delve into the mechanisms and recent improvements in DSSCs. Gong et al. (2017) discuss device modeling, cutting-edge techniques, and novel device structures aming for low input and high efficiency. Khan et al. (2017) examine strategies to improve efficiency and stability, particularly focusing on device layer architecture, dye selection, and electrolyte choice. Ahmad et al. (2017) explore the development of TiO2 photoanodes, covering improvement approaches and fabrication methods, albeit with limited coverage of other components. These reviews tend to emphasize optimization techniques rather than the impact of specific strategies and often overlook in-depth summaries of high-quality research on nanomaterials. This review aims to bridge these gaps by analyzing the impact of nanomaterials on various functional areas in DSSCs, specifically investigating how the type and morphology of nanomaterials influence the performance of DSSCs in each functional area. The main research concerns include photoelectric conversion efficiency, environmental benefits, economic benefits, and long-term stability. The optimal nanomaterials are selected for each functional area based on quantitative comparison. The ideal DSSC structure with nanomaterials is finally proposed by analyzing different functional areas. The advantages, disadvantages, and enhancement mechanisms for nanomaterials are summarized step by step. This step-by-step quantitative analysis offers valuable guides to optimally tailor the type, size, and morphology of nanomaterials for the critical components in a DSSC.

This research establishes a strategic orientation for DSSCs within integrated energy systems, contributing to breakthroughs in DSSCs and promoting the use of renewable and sustainable energy. Through intuitive and reliable quantitative analysis, this study solidifies the robustness of its conclusions, although it does not precisely define the impact level of nanomaterial properties on the photoelectric performance of DSSCs. The research primarily highlights the role of nanomaterials, with less discussion on the effects of base materials and their synergistic interactions with nanomaterials in each functional area. This focus aims to enhance the photoelectric performance of DSSCs by introducing nanomaterials into various components. Recommendations are provided for the selection of appropriate nanomaterials and nanostructures tailored to different components. Additionally, the mechanisms through which these enhancements occur are explained in detail, along with the physicochemical characteristics of the nanomaterials or nanostructures used. Overall, this review analyses the photoelectric efficiency of DSSCs from a novel perspective, offering insights into potential avenues for further improvements in their performance.

DSSCs mimic the principles of photosynthesis, employing light-absorbing dyes to capture photons and convert them to electrical energy. This approach offers a range of advantages, including minimal initial investment, environmental cleanliness, simple preparation, low toxicity, and easy production.

As depicted in Fig. 2 , the fundamental architecture of DSSCs consists of three pivotal regions, i.e., the photoanode, the electrolyte, and the photocathode. The photoanode employs transparent conductive oxide (TCO) glass (e.g., fluorine-doped tin oxide, FTO), coated by thin-layer mesoporous semiconductor oxides such as titanium dioxide (TiO2), stannic oxide (SnO2), and zinc oxide (ZnO) (Ahmad and Kim, 2023). The thin film of semiconductor oxide is loaded with dye molecules as light-adsorbing photosensitizers. DSSCs adopt a variety of photosensitizers, ranging from metal-organic complexes to metal-free organic compounds and natural dyes, effectively facilitating photon absorption (Sen et al., 2023). The electrolyte often uses a triiodide/iodide redox couple solution (
I 3 −
/
I −
) to regenerate dye molecules. Solid or quasi-solid electrolytes also show great potential, which can mitigate the stability concerns and safety risks often associated with liquid electrolytes. On the photocathode side, the counter electrode, usually employing TCO coated by metal platinum (Pt), facilitate the catalysis process. Fig. 2 illustrates the envisioned attributes across the diverse functional layers, shaping the blueprint for an idealized cell performance.

Visible light is absorbed by the sensitizer dyes, leading to photoexcitation of the dye and a series of electron transport processes. Photoelectron is first injected into the semiconductor film from the excited state of the dye. The dye regeneration is conducted by the electron donation from the
I −
/ I
3 −
redox system in the electrolyte. The redox system is regenerated by the electrons at the counter electrode flow through the external circuit. It has to be mentioned that photoinjected electrons in the semiconductor film may also simultaneously recombine with the oxidized dye or with the redox couple. As a result, control of charge separation and recombination is critical for DSSCs. Charge separation is primarily driven by the inherent energetics (redox potentials) of the different species at the semiconductor/dye/electrolyte interface rather than by the presence of macroscopic, electrostatic, potential energy gradients. This working principle is illustrated in Fig. 3 and described more quantitatively.

The photoelectric conversion in DSSCs is theoretically achieved through the processes. As previously mentioned, recombination between the semiconductor conduction electrons and oxidized dyes, as well as oxidized ions from the electrolytes, occurs simultaneously, as follows.

The entire system returns to its initial state through this cyclic process, as shown in Fig. 3. For DSSCs to achieve high photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE), the rates of electron injection and transport surpass that of electron recombination. Angelis (De Angelis et al., 2011) measured kinetic parameters of critical processes in a DSSC using N719 dye-sensitized titanium dioxide nanocrystal as the photoanode and
I −
/ I
3 −
as the REDOX pairs. The findings revealed an electron injection efficiency exceeding 99.9%. Furthermore, the recovery reaction rate of dye-positive ions with reduced ions in the electrolyte outpaces the rate of recombination with the electrons in the semiconductor by a factor of 100, as depicted in Fig. 3(b). The efficiency of solar cells is also influenced by factors such as light harvesting efficiency, hole injection efficiency, hole transport efficiency, and hole capture efficiency. The characteristics of heterojunction interfaces similarly impact electron transport performance. The movement of electrons in DSSCs can be categorized into migration and diffusion, as shown in Fig. 3(c). Electron diffusion primarily occurs near the electrode, where a significant concentration difference exists. Conversely, electron migration occurs within the electrolyte and is heavily affected by potential differences. Electron migration also encounters resistance at heterojunction interfaces within DSSCs, including interfaces between the conductive substrate and the semiconductor, between the semiconductor and dye molecules, between dye molecules and the electrolyte, and finally between the electrolyte and the counter electrode.

The assessment of solar cell performance primarily resolves around the electrical performance test, which focuses mainly on PCE. Electric performance parameters encompass key metrics such as open-circuit voltage (V oc), short-circuit current density (J sc), and fill factor (FF). J
sc represents the output current of a solar cell when the output voltage is zero, and V
os denotes the output voltage of the solar cell without load. Robust J sc and elevated V oc are the cornerstones of superior PCE. The photocurrent-voltage characteristic curve of the DSSC intuitively illustrates the interplay among these parameters, as shown in Fig. 4 (Shahiduzzaman et al., 2022).

The FF of solar cells is the ratio of the actual maximum output power to the theoretical maximum output power, as determined by Eq. (8):
