ce:section.ce:section-title: Important breakthrough in the development of DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Before 1991, DSSCs exhibited a PCE of less than 1%. This limitation primarily stemmed from the minimal dye adsorption on the planar photoanode surface. In 1991, O’Regan and Grätzel (1991) revolutionized DSSC fabrication by introducing an optically transparent film of titanium dioxide nanoparticles to replace the flat photoanode. This innovation led to a remarkable surge in PCE, surpassing 7%, attributed to the large surface area offered by the TiO2 nanoparticle semiconductor film and the ideal spectral characteristics of the Ruthenium dye. Following this pivotal moment, integrating nanocrystalline mesoporous semiconductor films into photoanodes of DSSCs became commonplace. Leveraging nanomaterials and nanotechnology successfully addressed the underlying technical challenges previously limiting DSSC efficiency. In 1993, the research group of Gratzel improved DSSC performance, observing a PCE exceeding 10%, which is closer to the efficiency levels observed in traditional silicon photovoltaic cells at that time (Nazeeruddin et al., 1993).

It constitutes a multifaceted optimization endeavor in the progression of DSSCs. A pivotal milestone emerged in 1998 when the inaugural solid-state DSSCs were manufactured by replacing the liquid electrolyte with amorphous organic material, yielding a maximum incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) of 33% (Bach et al., 1998). The integration of solid-state electrolytes serves as a remedy for the volatility challenges inherent in liquid electrolytes. However, solid electrolytes introduce higher internal and interface resistances than their liquid counterparts. To bridge this disparity, quasi-solid-state electrolytes with polymer gels were introduced, combining the benefits of both liquid and solid electrolytes. Ultramicroelectrode voltammetric measurements demonstrated unhindered charge transport of the triiodide/iodide (
I 3 −
/
I −
) couple within the polymer gel (Wang et al., 2003). Through a comprehensive exploration of the electronic transport mechanisms inside DSSCs, nanofillers have been introduced into polymer gel electrolytes to accelerate electron transport and reduce charge recombination.

Bifacial DSSCs are also designed to generate high electric power through static solar concentrators. An additional advantage lies in their inherent adaptability to varying sunlight directions. In the work of Ito et al. (2008), a silicon dioxide (SiO2) layer was added between the TiO2 and Pt electrodes to prevent the generation of undesired reverse currents, eliminating charge recombination. The idea of regulating the electron transport pathway has been used to improve the photoelectric conversion performance of DSSCs. Meanwhile, the pore density of porous semiconductor films remains a pivotal factor affecting electron transport performance. To address challenges like silver erosion and partially visible light absorption of the triiodide/iodide couple, novel redox couples have been embraced. Wang et al. (2010a) achieved a groundbreaking efficiency of 6.4% for a DSSC utilizing the disulfide/thiolate redox couple under standard illumination test conditions. Further advancements have been realized using cobalt complexes and ferrocene/ferrocenium single-electron mediators (Daeneke et al., 2011; Yum et al., 2012). These mediators exhibit minimal absorption within the visible range of the solar spectrum, with their redox properties modulated through the strategic selection of donor/acceptor substituents on the ligand.

A PCE of 12.3% was achieved for a DSSC through a combination of a cobalt-complex redox mediator (Co(II/III)tris(bipyridyl)) and co-sensitization of porphyrin dyes (Yella et al., 2011). Furthermore, a cp-sensitized DSSC exhibited a PCE of 15.2% by pre-adsorbing hydroxamic acid derivatives on the nano-mesoporous TiO2 semiconductor films (Ren et al., 2023). The presence of this hydroxamic acid monolayer introduced a decelerating effect on dye molecule uptake. Consequently, it inhibited the desorption of co-sensitizers, facilitating enhanced packing density and improved ordering of the dye monolayer. Mathew et al. (2014) also reorganized porphyrin dye by molecular engineering to improve the issues of electrolyte compatibility and light-harvesting properties. A high PCE of 13% for DSSCs was obtained by incorporating a cobalt(II/III) redox shuttle with a single porphyrin dye. In addition, minimizing electron resistance across heterogeneous interfaces is pivotal in enhancing photovoltaic performance. To suppress charge recombination, the integration of multi-functional interface modification materials has been introduced, showcasing promising outcomes. Cao et al. (2018) designed an advanced DSSC architecture to mitigate Warburg resistance through the direct contact of mesoporous TiO2 photoanode and poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) counter electrode, which achieved a notable PCE of 13.1% with Cu(II)/Cu(I) redox liquid electrolyte.

Fig. 5 shows the significant strides witnessed in the evolution of DSSCs from 1991 to 2022. However, achieving breakthroughs in performance and functionality through electrode modification has proven a formidable challenge. Over 30 years of dedicated development, DSSCs have incrementally aligned with high efficiency, stability, environmental sustainability, and cost-effectiveness demands. In this trajectory, nanomaterials have been pivotal in advancing cutting-edge photoanodes, interface enhancements, and the realization of quasi-solid state electrolytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic contributions of nanomaterials to the advancement of DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have integrated themselves into pivotal domains of DSSC fabrication, such as reddish-orange Sm3+ doped BaGd2ZnO5 (Sheoran et al., 2022) and Sm3+-activated YAG nano-crystalline (Singh et al., 2021). At the heart of nanomaterial behavior lie fundamental traits such as quantum size effect, small size effect, surface effect (interface effect), and quantum tunneling effect. These intrinsic features impact the unique physical and chemical properties of nanomaterials, endowing them with exceptional characteristics. As a result, advanced materials, particularly nanomaterials, hold the potential to elevate DSSC performance across diverse dimensions.

Quantum size effect When a particle's size falls below a specific nanometer threshold, its electronic energy levels, particularly those near the Fermi level, undergo a transformation from quasi-continuous to discrete. This phenomenon, known as the quantum size effect, is frequently observed as either energy level splitting or the widening of energy gaps. This effect creates distinct physical properties that deviate significantly from the macroscopic state, thereby offering novel prospects within the nanoelectronics field. A notable manifestation of the quantum size effect is the phenomenon termed the “blue shift”, wherein the absorption edge and photoluminescence peak shift towards shorter wavelengths as the nanocrystal size decreases. This spectral shift underscores the capacity to modulate band structures by manipulating nanomaterial morphologies. One illustrative example is found in nanomaterials like quantum dots, which exhibit the ability to absorb light across diverse wavelengths, thereby expanding the spectrum of absorbed light that can be used for electricity generation. A semiconductor quantum dot, characterized by a remarkable quantum size effect, emerges as a mesoscopic system of significance. This effect has been applied prominently in quantum dot-sensitized solar cells, where quantum dots substantially enhance the absorption of visible light compared to conventional dyes.

Small size effect The small size effect arises from the disruption of periodic boundary conditions in materials (in the case of crystals) or the reduction of atomic density near the surface layer (for non-crystals). This effect occurs when a material's size shrinks below a specific characteristic scale, which refers to the wavelength of light waves, the de Broglie wavelength, and the coherence length or projection depth of the superconducting state. Notably, it gives rise to the plasmon resonance phenomenon within nanomaterials, driven by their reduced dimensions. This phenomenon promotes solar radiation absorption by stimulating intense plasmonic resonances.

Quantum-size and small-size effects have contrasting impacts on the physical properties and underlying conditions. The conventional physical characteristics such as optical, electrical, magnetic, and thermodynamic properties are influenced by the small size effect. For example, when materials are minified to the nanoscale, metals take on a black appearance, and magnetic nanomaterials exhibit superparamagnetism. In contrast, the quantum size effect causes nanomaterials to exhibit entirely distinct magnetic, optical, thermal, and electrical properties compared to conventionally sized counterparts. The most obvious evidence of this effect is the narrowing and blue shift of the absorption spectrum band. The small size effect comes into play when the periodic boundaries break down, and it generally applies to most materials with a size below 100 nm. In contrast, the critical size for the quantum size effect is related to energy level splitting, and it varies depending on the specific material in question.

Surface effect Among the prominent attributes of the surface effect, the rapid increases in surface area and surface energy take center stage. This surge is a direct consequence of the notable spike in the ratio of surface atoms to the total atom count. Resulting from this phenomenon, nanoparticles exhibit higher unsaturated and suspended bonds, rendering them exceptionally prone to chemical interactions. This propensity for reactivity is rooted in the diminished atomic coordination at the surface. One notable outcome of the surface effect is the broadening of bond vibration modes, contributing to the expansion of the infrared absorption band within nanoparticles. Disorderliness in atom arrangement characterizes the surface of nanomaterials, endowing them with malleability and ductility arising from atom migration under external deformation. This enhanced toughness and ductility promote the advancement of flexible DSSCs. Furthermore, nanomaterials can improve the interfaces between the various layers in the solar cell structure, such as electron and hole transporting layers. This enhancement proves instrumental in facilitating efficient carrier transfer, reducing carrier recombination, and improving the overall efficiency of solar cells.

Tunneling effect The tunneling effect pertains to the remarkable capability of microscopic particles to penetrate barriers that would normally seem insurmountable. The use of nanomaterials can also create this tunneling effect, enabling electrons to navigate through small gaps between materials. This process facilitates highly efficient electron transport by eliminating the need for electrons to traverse the bulk of the material, which could otherwise induce issues like recombination or other undesirable losses.

In summary, the fundamental effects of nanomaterials collectively promote significant changes in their thermal, optical, mechanical, and electrophysical properties. These distinct properties propel nanomaterials towards enhancing the photoelectric conversion performance of solar cells. Their influence spans a spectrum of factors, including charge separation, electron transport, interlayer connectivity, electrolyte stability, and device resilience. Efforts are concentrated on scrutinizing nanomaterials to optimize DSSC performance. The diverse impacts of nanomaterial integration on the photoanode, electrolyte, and counter electron in DSSCs are extensively examined and presented. Based on this, an ideal DSSC configuration is conceptually proposed, in which the optimal type, size, and morphology of nanomaterials are specially tailored for its key components.

This section describes the process of electron transport in the photoanode of DSSCs. The pivotal factor for enhancing DSSC performance lies in photoelectron efficiency. This efficiency can be improved by controlling dye properties to broaden optical response, modifying interface to impede charge recombination, and aligning energy levels between dyes and semiconductor films. The quantum of captured photons is directly affected by dye properties. Furthermore, the pivotal factor of diffusion resistance at the photoanode impacts the PCE of solar cells. In this context, using nanomaterials and nanotechnology shows promising potential for expediting electron transport and accommodating higher dye loads through adept preparation and morphology modification of semiconductor films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potentials of nanomaterials in mesoporous semiconductor films
ce:section.ce:para: The choice of semiconductor film materials involves a thoughtful consideration of factors such as energy levels, band gap width, size, morphology, crystal structure, and surface characteristics. When forming mesoporous semiconductor films, nanomaterials serve vital roles, encompassing photoanode dimensional adjustment, surface modification, and the creation of composite photoanodes. TiO2 (Cui et al., 2015; Salam et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2022; Yao et al., 2013; Zamiri et al., 2021), ZnO (Anand et al., 2022; Bicer et al., 2022), and tin oxide (SnO2) (Dou et al., 2011; Niu and He, 2015) are the commonly used materials to synthesize nano-porous semiconductor films in DSSCs. Structures with TiO2 and ZnO are always the focus of discussion (Boro et al., 2018).

Among different materials, nano-crystalline TiO2 DSSCs notably exhibit the highest photoelectric efficiency. The optoelectronic attributes of solar cells featuring TiO2 film are improved by elevated temperatures, impact compaction (Yao et al., 2013), and in-situ passivation (Dong et al., 2020). Chu et al. (2018) used a template-free approach to synthesize mesoporous anatase TiO2 nanomaterials (MATNs) characterized by interconnected nanoparticles. The utilization of MATNs as photoelectrodes yields an exceptional optoelectronic efficiency of up to 7.78% in DSSCs. In addition, the introduction of Ag/TiO2 composite nanomaterials in DSSCs results in a staggering 15-fold amplification in optoelectronic efficiency compared to pristine TiO2 nanomaterial cells. This surge can be attributed to the dual action of low-probability recombination and rapid transport of electrons at interfaces modified by the nanomaterial (Nair et al., 2018). Similarly, Ag/reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/TiO2 materials contribute to a photoelectric efficiency of 6.87%, a remarkable 15% augmentation over pure TiO2 nanoparticles (Javed et al., 2019). In addition, Low and Lai (2018) reviewed the recent achievements for graphene-TiO2 composite nanostructured-based photoanode in DSSCs. The advancements in composite TiO2 nanomaterials’ performance as photoanodes for DSSCs are primarily underpinned by alterations in band gap characteristics (Ullah et al., 2021). Moreover, the incorporation of transition metals such as Cu, Cr, and Co into TiO2 matrices has been explored, leading to improved PCE in transition metals composite TiO2-based DSSCs. This enhancement benefits from the mitigation of charge transfer resistance, reductions in crystallite size, and enhancements in dispersion, surface area, and electrical conductivity.

Due to the prominent scattering capacity and rapid electron transport property, one-dimensional nanostructures emerge as compelling candidates for DSSC photoanode fabrication. Among these, TiO2 nanotubes have shown considerable promise, achieving a notable conversion efficiency of 5.03%, surpassing both TiO2 nanoparticles and TiO2 nanorods in DSSCs (Arla et al., 2022). The ordered alignment intrinsic to one-dimensional TiO2 nanostructures establishes a direct electron transport channel between the semiconductor film and the conductive substrate. Remarkably, the incorporation of Ag Cr-doped TiO2 nanotubes significantly reduces the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers (Nguyen et al., 2020). This is exemplified by a DSSC achieving a remarkable PCE of 8.69% via the utilization of a semiconductor film composed of Ag–Cr-doped TiO2 nanotubes. However, it is worth noting that an excessive Cr concentration within the TiO2 nanotubes results in the structural degradation of the nano-tubular framework, as reported in (Momeni, 2017).

Multi-dimensional nanostructures have found application as photoanode materials in DSSCs (Zhang et al., 2024). For instance, fluorine-doped TiO2 nanostructures are synthesized using a microwave-assisted solvothermal technique, yielding a quasi-cubic morphology that translates to a PCE of 8.2% in DSSCs (Madurai Ramakrishnan et al., 2021). In another study, Lv et al. (2019) successfully synthesized hierarchical cotton-like TiO2 nanomaterials (HCTN). The utilization of HCTN in DSSCs leads to a remarkable transformation efficiency of 8.9%, a noteworthy 62% enhancement compared to the untreated P25 photoanode. This performance boost is attributed to the efficient propagation of electrons along the slimmer 1D TiO2 nanowires, thereby contributing to the superior DSSC performance. The mechanisms behind the improved photoelectric performance afforded by different nanostructures have been analyzed in detail in the above discussion. An intuitive comparison of the evaluation parameters, such as open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current, fill factor, and photoelectric efficiency, for DSSCs employing TiO2 mesoporous semiconductor films is shown in Table 1 . This table indicates that the element doping and complex nanostructure improve the photoelectric efficiency of TiO2 nanoparticles or nanotubes at the most (except for the Cr-doped TiO2 nanotube). Table 1 highlights the meticulous control over morphology and strategic element doping of the TiO2 semiconductor film, which yields marked improvements in the photoelectric conversion performance of the DSSCs across the board.

Shaping the morphology of nanomaterials is an emerging technique to improve photoanode performance in DSSC applications (Abrari et al., 2024). A notable illustration of this lies in the fabrication of ZnO nanoflakes (ZnO-Nfs) via a hydrothermal process, yielding the characteristic hexagonal wurtzite structure (Han et al., 2018). The synthesized ZnO-Nfs, adopted as a photoanode in DSSCs, behave with an excellent conversion efficiency of approximately 3.39%. This performance enhancement was caused by the flake-like morphology and the amplified surface area intrinsic to ZnO-Nfs, both of which promote the photoelectric conversion process. Furthermore, the incorporation of three-dimensional hierarchical nanostructures contributes to an optimized dye placement, enhanced radiation absorption capability, and expedited electron mobility, which result in superior DSSC performance.

The flower-like nanostructure is assembled by ZnO nanorods as photoanodes to achieve superior photoconversion efficiency in DSSCs. The ZnO nanorods with a high aspect ratio improve the photoconversion efficiency, resulting in an increase of nearly 2.5-fold compared to their lower aspect ratio counterparts (Zhang et al., 2014). In comparison to conventional synthesis methods, the microwave synthesis route or the seed layer-hydrothermal method shows distinct advantages in terms of reaction duration and the uniformity of nanostructures (Bicer et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation method for the mesoporous semiconductor film
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows various preparation methods for crafting nanostructured photoanodes, including vapor-phase, liquid-phase, and solid-phase methods. Vapor-phase approaches encompass pyrolysis, oxidation, and hydrolysis, while liquid-phase techniques encompass sol-gel (Marami and Farahmandjou, 2019), hydrothermal (Seo et al., 2020), chemical precipitation (Toloman et al., 2019), and microemulsion methods (Pineda-Aguilar et al., 2020). A prime example of the hydrothermal method's effectiveness lies in introducing multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) into TiO2, reducing charge transport resistance within the cell. This reduction contributes to increased overall charge transporting efficiency and improved electric conductivity (Seo et al., 2020). The sol-gel method, involving the transformation of sol to gel, yields materials endowed with desired chemical and physic-mechanical properties. Employing the sol-gel technique, TiO2@C composites have exhibited remarkable visible-light photocatalytic performance due to the presence of carbon within the composites (Wang et al., 2012). Compared to the vapor-phase approach, the liquid-phase preparation method offers a simple process characterized by lower energy consumption, higher precision, and lower cost. The solid-phase method includes ball milling (Malevu, 2021) and ultrasonic dispersion (Rosiles-Perez et al., 2020), further expanding the toolkit of fabrication strategies. Fabrication methods for TiO2 photoanodes are given with careful retrospect in the review of Ahmad et al. (2017), and they described the working principle and necessary techniques based on physical and chemical methods in detail.

Optimal sensitizers must satisfy an array of criteria, including a broad spectral response range (with a band gap width of 1.5 eV), a high extinction coefficient, the presence of anchoring groups, and a suitable HOMO/LUMO energy level. In addition, the dyes should exhibit the indispensable attributes of enduring thermal and chemical stability under continuous irradiation over 20 years. Unfortunately, to date, no investigation indicates the attainment of the stringent 20-year continuous irradiation criterion through synthetic dyes. In DSSCs, the prevailing categories of sensitizers include pure organic and metallic organic complex dyes, both extensively employed for their respective merits.

Polypyridyl ruthenium complex-based dyes possess the advantages of exceptional stability, rapid electron injection rates, and high PCE. However, the limitation imposed by the costly and scarce nature of ruthenium hampers their broader application in DSSCs. Pure organic dyes emerge as excellent alternatives to their metal-organic counterparts, boasting easy synthesis, cost-effectiveness, and elevated extinction coefficients. Typically, adopting a D-π-A structure, pure organic dyes enable flexible absorptivity adjustments through adept modifications of electron donor (D), electron acceptor (A), and conjugated bridge (π) molecular structures. Other cheap and non-toxic alternatives with excellent performance have been investigated widely. Magnetic nanomaterials bearing amine groups, e.g., Fe3O4@SiO2@IPA, are promising solar cell sensitizers (Kutluay et al., 2021). Notably, the spinel-structured Fe3O4@SiO2@IPA nanomaterial boasts a band gap of 2.48 eV. In parallel, copper complexes have emerged as notable replacements for ruthenium-based dyes (Conradie, 2022).

Advancements in natural pigments have resulted in a viable and economical substitute for ruthenium dyes as photosensitizers in DSSCs (Arsyad et al., 2024; Iman et al., 2024). These natural dyes, extracted through a simple process, offer safety and environmental compatibility and eliminate the need for expensive chemical synthesis (Richhariya et al., 2017). As reported, most DSSCs utilizing natural dyes exhibit lower photoelectric conversion efficiencies compared to their ruthenium-based counterparts. For instance, DSSCs incorporating four natural dyes from Ixora floral sources have photoelectric efficiencies of less than 1% (Wazzan et al., 2022). Based on the review of Richhariya et al. (2017), the most efficient natural dye derived from sumac/rhus fruits only provides a PCE of 1.5% for DSSCs. The PCE of natural dye-based DSSCs is primarily limited by the single morphology and fixed valence band of the sensitizers. Nanoparticles are often used as a secondary layer to improve the performance of natural dye-based DSSCs. A water-based DSSC achieved a PCE of 1.49% using a natural dye by depositing gold nanoparticles on a TiO2 electrode, forming a Schottky barrier (Lai et al., 2008). The addition of gold nanoparticles improved the adsorptive properties of natural dyes in the photoanode. Miao et al. (2022) improved the performance of DSSCs using natural blueberry juice as sensitizers by depositing a nano-SnS layer and gold nanoparticles on the TiO2 photoanode. The synthetic structure provided a smaller direct bandgap to enhance the absorption and utilization of visible light. Furthermore, nanomaterials can be adopted as co-sensitizers with natural dyes, broadening the spectral response range. Semiconductor nanomaterials like quantum dots are promising candidates as sensitizers for DSSCs. The integration of quantum dots has been explored to suppress the recombination of electrons and holes, thereby enhancing the electron injection rate from the sensitizers into the semiconductor film (Zheng and Zhang, 2021). However, the use of heavy metals-based quantum dots presents challenges due to their instability and intricate synthesis (Riaz et al., 2019). The limited affinity of graphene quantum dots for the TiO2 surface restricts their application in DSSCs (Mahalingam et al., 2022). In contrast, carbon quantum dots with nitrogen doping have been developed, exhibiting excellent interface contact characteristics and favorable optical properties (Riaz et al., 2019; Shejale et al., 2021).

To further enhance its efficiency, precise control over the assembly of dye molecules on the mesoporous semiconductor film is essential, favoring optimal charge generation. The quantity of loaded dyes determines the upper limit of radiation absorption within specific spectral ranges. Theoretically, an augmented amount of loaded dyes correlates with improved photoelectric conversion performance, as given in Fig. 8 (a–c). However, the possibility of aggregation also increases with increased dye loading, leading to a degradation of photoelectric conversion performance, as given in Fig. 8(d). The overall performance of solar cells is intricately affected by other variables, such as photoanode surface properties and the contact conditions between photosensitizers and photoanodes. The transfer resistance of electrons between the semiconductor film and dye molecules is affected by interface conditions, as shown in Fig. 8(e). The elimination of organic residues from the titanium (IV) oxide surface elevates the PCE of a DSSC from 4.59% to 5.89%, possibly due to the introduction of heterojunction interfaces (Augustowski et al., 2021). In addition, the non-compact arrangement of dye molecules can facilitate the impregnation of the electrolyte into the semiconductor film, thereby increasing opportunities for the recombination of photogenerated electrons, as depicted in Fig. 8(g). The surface modification or passivation of the semiconductor film effectively avoids photogenerated electron recombination caused by direct contact with the electrolyte. One approach involves utilizing semiconductor nanomaterials with core-shell structures, an avenue aligned with nanoporous film preparation. Alternatively, the formation of a compact molecular layer by incorporating organic compounds in electrolytes, as shown in Fig. 8(h) (Zhang et al., 2013), presents another avenue. Studies have reported the significant enhancement of DSSC conversion efficiency, with some achieving a remarkable 17.2% improvement through the introduction of a lithium carbonate passivation layer (Zhang and Zaban, 2008). Similarly, a 3.5-fold enhancement over the pristine SnO2 photoanode is reported when an electronic insulating layer of calcium carbonate (CaCO3) is integrated (Shaikh et al., 2015). Co-sensitized configurations show an innovative strategy to improve cell performance (AlGhamdi et al., 2020; Mahalingam et al., 2022), where spectral complementary matching between various dyes aims for comprehensive spectrum absorption, as shown in Fig. 8(f). Furthermore, different structures contribute to the ordered arrangement of dye molecules on the semiconductor film. For instance, cubic carbon quantum dots (4.87 nm), synthesized via hydrothermal conversion from gelidium amansii powders, resulting in a noteworthy PCE of 8.68% for a DSSC tailored with CCQDs/N719. This figure surpasses that of the N719-only device (a PCE of 7.83%) (Dou et al., 2018). Advancing the frontier of fluorescent nanostructures, a novel approach resolves around the quantum dot core of Cu–In–S augmented with a layer of ZnS (Amaral-Junior et al., 2021). Mesoporous semiconductor films emerge as a solution to address the challenge of insufficient dye loading with a small specific surface area.

Semiconductor nanomaterials, notably TiO2 and ZnO, are widely adopted in the fabrication of photoanodes for both DSSCs and quantum dot-sensitized solar cells. A paramount objective is to align the photoanode's absorption spectrum with the solar emission spectrum, a critical stride in enhancing optical utilization efficiency. Material, doping, morphology, and film-thickness have always been considered as the critical influencing factors for the performance of photoanodes (Sengupta et al., 2016), and the diagram of these factors is shown in Fig. 9 . The pursuit of an expanded spectral response range propels the integration of diverse nanomaterial morphologies and doping species, with the resultant doping ratios (or combination ratios) intricately influencing solar cell performance. Equally consequential are factors like photoanode surface roughness and area, which impact dye and quantum dot loadings. Innovative nanomaterials and nanostructures promote the advancement of high-performance mesoporous semiconductor films characterized by expansive specific surface areas and rapid charge transport rates.

The role of sensitizer, crucial for facilitating electron injection into the optical absorption layer, enables effective charge separation. Although ruthenium-based dyes (an efficiency above 10%) show excellent performance in fulfilling sensitizer requirements, their scarcity and high costs have promoted the exploration of alternatives. Among these alternatives, natural dyes with a simple extraction process are cost-effective and environmentally friendly due to their high molar extinction coefficient and abundant availability. However, the efficiency of natural dyes-based solar cells has been limited by issues such as dye decomposition over time, leading to instability within the solar cell system (Yahya et al., 2021). It is anticipated that advancements in the performance of natural dyes could yield significant accomplishments in both economic and environmental aspects. Quantum dots, renowned for their distinct optical properties, have emerged as potent sensitizers for solar cells, fostering the development of novel solar cell systems. As this technology continues to evolve, it holds the promise of further advancing solar energy harvesting and conversion. The photoanode of DSSCs is composed of mesoporous semiconductor films and sensitizers. However, existing studies often focus on optimizing these two components independently, neglecting their potential synergistic effects on photoelectric performance. As a result, there may be a suboptimal combination of potential candidates within the photoanode. In addition, while various nanostructures have been investigated for the preparation of mesoporous semiconductors, nano-hollow spheres have been largely overlooked despite their significant potential in photoelectric applications. Changes in nanostructures, such as these hollow spheres, can alter both the band gap of the nanomaterials and the porosity of the mesoporous semiconductor films. However, the relationship between these structural changes and the hole transport rate remains unexplored. By investigating this relationship, valuable insights can be gained into enhancing the performance of DSSCs.

Conventional liquid electrolytes with high ionic mobility have been widely used in DSSCs (Talip et al., 2024). Easy oxidation and corrosion of liquid electrolytes result in electrode breakage, cell leakage, high costs, and reductions in safety and stability. Quasi-solid or full-solid electrolytes are developed to solve the stability issue caused by the liquid electrolyte (Kaleem et al., 2022). Quasi-solid electrolyte meets the stability requirements of DSSCs, and the electron transport ability is better than that of a solid electrolyte. The gel material is usually adopted as a matrix of the quasi-solid electrolyte. Bio-sourced polymer matrix, xanthan gum, is adopted to form hydrogel electrolytes. By dipping conditions and redox mediator concentration, the cell efficiency in hydrogel electrolytes is improved by 20% after a 1200-h aging test, 2.7% superior to 100% aqueous electrolytes in overall performance (Galliano et al., 2020). A polymer gel electrolyte is synthesized by mixing liquid electrolytes with iodide/triiodide and solid crystalline biopolymer. The polymer gel electrolyte provides long-term stability over 15 days (Gunasekaran et al., 2022). To strengthen the photoelectric efficiency and stability in DSSCs, pristine insulator natural rubber latex is an additive agent to the liquid redox mediator with iodide/triiodide. The principal reason for the improved photoelectric efficiency and stability is the suppression of the countermovement of electrons due to the introduction of rubber (Kamenan et al., 2021). Compared with liquid electrolytes, the stability of DSSCs improves by 3–5% for the quasi-solid electrolyte (Cakar et al., 2021).

Nanofillers have been added to optimize the ion transport property of the gel polymer electrolyte (Farhana et al., 2024). A DSSC with quasi-solid electrolytes keeps 91.4% of its premier efficiency after 2 h, whereas a DSSC with liquid electrolytes keeps only 73.2% (Chawarambwa et al., 2021). The enhanced stability of a DSSC with a quasi-solid electrolyte is attributed to a 3D nano frame, effectively preventing the evaporation and divulgation of electrolytes. In addition, insufficient segmental motion within the gel polymer also leads to a low transport rate. The ionic conductivity and ion diffusion rates have been improved by adding a nanofiller in the gel polymer electrolyte, which was caused by the reduced crystallinity of the polymer and low charge recombination resistance of the photoanode and electrolyte interface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gel polymer electrolyte with metal oxide nano-filler
ce:section.ce:para: TiO2, ZnO, SiO2 and manganese oxide (MnO2) and cobalt oxide (Co3O4), aluminum oxide (Al2O3), and copper oxide (CuO) are the widely used metal oxide nano filler in gel polymer electrolytes.

TiO2 nanomaterials TiO2 nanoparticles were first used as fillers of poly (vinylidene fluoride-hexafluoropropylene) (PVDF-HFP) to synthesize nanocomposite gel electrolytes for DSSCs (Huo et al., 2007). The apparent diffusion coefficients of triiodide reached 4.42 × 10−10 m2/s in gel electrolytes with TiO2 nanofillers due to the action of the Grotthuss mechanism. Moreover, a DSSC with the TiO2 nanoparticles-based gel electrolytes maintains 90% of initial conversion efficiency after accelerated aging tests with heating at 60 °C for 1000 h, much higher than the DSSCs with liquid electrolytes. TiO2 nanotubes were first introduced into polyethylene glycol (PEG) to prepare composite gel electrolytes for DSCCs in 2007, which reached a PCE of ∼4.43% (Akhtar et al., 2007). The morphological properties of the composite electrolyte are mainly affected by the interaction between TiO2 nanotubes and PEG. Moreover, the ratio of TiO2 nanotubes and PEG influences the interaction between TiO2 nanotubes and PEG. The authors clarified the internal mechanism of the nano-filler amount in the electrolyte on the photoelectric conversion performance. A hybrid solid-state electrolyte is prepared by dispersing one-dimensional TiO2 in a mixed polyethylene glycol matrix. The solid-state electrolyte yields a PCE of 6.1% (Lim et al., 2021). 1D TiO2 nanofiller results in improvements in light capture and charge transport. Mallikarjun et al. (2022) also revealed that the polymer electrolyte shows a smooth texture and an interconnecting network of micro porous nature after introducing TiO2 nanoparticles through SEM analysis. The DSCC with Mg2+ ion-based PVDF-HFP was observed a PCE of 9.9%, which is caused by the crystal phase transition of polymer from (α + β) to (β + γ) under the action of TiO2 nanoparticles.

ZnO nanomaterials An PCE of 7.33% had been reported for a DSSC with polymer electrolytes with the action of the interaction of ZnO nanoparticles, sodium iodide salt, PVDF-HFP, and polyethylene oxide (PEO) polymers (Zebardastan et al., 2017). The amorphous phase of polymer electrolytes was enhanced due to the introduction of ZnO nanoparticles. In addition, the increment in ionic conductivity of nanocomposite polymer electrolytes was caused by the created ion transmittal channels with ZnO nanoparticles. Shape-controlled ZnO nanostructures had been used as nanofillers of polymer gel electrolytes for a DSSC with mesoporous carbon electrocatalyst (Kim et al., 2021). Maximum PCE values of 6.4% and 5.4% were respectively found at the DSSCs with rod-shaped petals (ZnO nanorod) and almond-shaped petals (ZnO nano almond) nanocomposite polymer gel electrolytes, which is higher than that of the pure polymer gel electrolytes (4.7%). The authors concluded that the improved photoelectrochemical performance was caused by the enhanced ion diffusion and scattering effect due to the practical path with ZnO nanomaterials for redox couple transfer. In addition, the rod-shaped petal structure leads to sufficient penetration of electrolytes compared to that of the almond-shaped petal structure, which results in a high photoelectric conversion performance for the DSSC with ZnO nanorods.

Co3O4 nanomaterials Transition metal oxide of Co3O4 shows strong Lewis acidity, facilitating the ion mobility by absorbing iodide ions into the electrolyte interface (Razali et al., 2023). The hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC)-based electrolytes with potassium iodide salt possess higher ionic conductivity compared to that with sodium iodide and lithium iodide slats (Goh et al., 2022). The ionic conductivity of the gel polymer electrolytes will be further improved with the addition of Co3O4 nanoparticles due to the reductions in activation energy and crystallinity degree. The matching of redox couple and nanofiller is also an important issue for improving the performance of quasi-solid electrolytes.

Other metal oxides Segmental motion within the nanocomposite gel polymer electrolyte has also been investigated from the physical structure of the nanofiller by adjusting the size and shape of CuO nanoparticles (Subramaniam et al., 2022). The structural order of the polymer chain was disrupted due to the shape change from rod to sphere, facilitating the redox couple transportation. Nano-silica was also introduced into a polymer matrix, and nano-silica was surface-modified with polyaniline to improve segmental motion and interface connection features (Sing Liow et al., 2022). Nanomaterials of hausmannite (Mn3O4) and MnO2 were also adopted as additives to improve the ion transport rate in the gel electrolytes, while only slight enhancements in PCE of DSSCs were found compared with that without nanofillers (Rani et al., 2022; Razali et al., 2023). However, a PCE value of 3.47% was measured when the nanocomposite of Mn3O4⋅CuS was introduced into the gel electrolyte-based DSSCs (Rani et al., 2022). In addition, quasi-solid DSSCs behave with a high PCE of 6.16% assembled with nanocomposite electrolytes of poly(propylene carbonate) (PPC) and Al3O4 nanoparticles (Choi et al., 2011).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gel polymer electrolyte with carbon nano-filler
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene and carbon nanotubes have revived the field of polymer nanocomposites due to their electrical conductivity and thermal stability (Kumar et al., 2024; Zafar et al., 2024). A PCE of 4.57% is obtained for a DSSC with polyvinyl acetate (PVAc)/graphene gel electrolyte, and the PCE value was higher than that of a DSSC with pure PVAc gel electrolyte (4.35%) and similar to the liquid electrolyte based DSSC (4.75%) (Rehman et al., 2020). However, the self-stacking phenomenon exists for graphene and carbon nanotubes with the action of the van der Waals force, leading to a significant reduction in electrical performance. The surface modification with heteroatoms functional groups for carbon nanomaterials improves the trapping ability of ions and the dispersion in the polymer matrix. For example, 15-crown-5-functionalized MWCNTs (MWCNT-15-C-5) and ionic liquids were used as electrolyte additives in a quasi-solid-state DSSC (Huang et al., 2011). The heterocyclic structure of the crown ether facilitates the dissolution of Li+ and I− in the electrolyte due to the capture of lithium ions (Li+) in the DSSC.

Generally, the electronic properties of graphene derivatives are inferior to that of pristine graphene due to residual functional groups. However, surface modification on graphene derivatives will promote the capture and diffusion of REDOX pairs in the electrolyte. For example, graphene oxide (GO) nanosheet-polyaniline nanohybrids serve as the electron transfer channels and catalysts of the reaction of electrochemical reduction I3 − (Hsu et al., 2014). Lansoprazole functionalized derivative was synthesized by covalently grafting 2-{(3-methyl-4-(2, 2, 2-trifluoroethoxy) pyridine-2-yl) methylsulfinyl}-1H-benzo(d)imidazole (lansoprazole, LNS) onto GO sheets (Chirani et al., 2022). LNS functional group induced N, S, O, and F atoms, which adjusts the electronic states of GO, resulting in the decrease of active sites for catalyzing I3 − to I−. In addition, fluorine atoms are used as the binding sites of I2 molecules. The intercalation structure, such as rGO incorporated between the layers of MXene, also prevents self-accumulation (Wen et al., 2022). Meanwhile, the ion diffusion rate increases with the action of the shortened distance between MXene and rGO.

Biopolymer nanofibrous membrane possesses a three-dimensional chambered network structure consisting of a high porosity index and numerous physical cross-linking points. In addition, the controllable fiber diameter and membrane porosity facilitate the penetration and uptake of liquid electrolytes to form the gel electrolyte (Dissanayake et al., 2023). Single-layered GO nanosheets were adopted as the addition of nanocomposite membranes to improve the electrical conductivity of the fibrous membrane by reducing the fiber mean diameter and the crystallinity degree of the polymer matrix (Abdollahi et al., 2021). Nanoclay is also a valuable substrate for preparing gel electrolytes, and it offers a high surface-to-volume ratio, outstanding ionic and thermal stability, and high UV light permeability (Gupta et al., 2021). However, uncertain components cause a poor matching between natural materials and quasi-solid electrolytes. The design of artificial nano-clay covers shortcomings in the properties of natural minerals to achieve high efficiencies (Pedro et al., 2016). The nanoclay increases thermal stability and suppresses the aging behavior of the electrolyte.

The liquid electrolyte faces problems like solvent leakages and dye desorption attached to the photoanode, which leads to the instability of the photoelectric conversion system. Quasi-solid and solid electrolyte electrolytes are synthesized to replace the liquid electrolytes of DSSCs. Nanoparticles in quasi-solid and full-solid electrolyte electrolytes contribute to establishing cross-linking networks between nanoparticles and polymer formation, facilitating ion transport within the polymer matrix. The electrical conductivity is improved by adding nanoparticles, but it may also lead to performance degradation due to the formation of nanoparticle aggregation. In addition, the properties of nanomaterial composite gel electrolytes are also affected by the type, morphology, and crystallinity of the nano-filler materials. For one definite nanofiller, the concentration and dispersion of the nanofiller determined the final performance of the gel electrolyte.

The coordination of the electrolyte matrix with the REDOX pair and nanomaterials is also crucial for electrolyte performance. Fig. 10 shows the potential of several common REDOX pairs based on the summary of Srivishnu et al. (2021). The potential of REDOX pairs should be lower than the ground state potential of dyes. Bonding between the surface-functional groups of the polymer and the nano-fillers is another influencing factor for charge transfer resistance in gel electrolytes. So far, many polymers and copolymers have been used to prepare gel electrolytes, such as poly(vinyl butyral-co-vinyl alcohol-co-vinyl acetate) (P(VBco-VA-co-VAc)), hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC), locust bean gum (LBG), γ-butyrolactone (GBL), PPC, PVDF-HFP, PEO, PEG, PVAc, etc. The effects of nanofiller-based electrolytes on the performance parameters of DSSCs are diverse. For example, carbon nanofillers show significant potential in improving photoelectric efficiency when used with a Pt photocathode. According to Table 2 , a high PCE of 9.99% is obtained using a PVDF-HFP polymer with TiO2 nanomaterials as the electrolyte and MWCNT as the counter electrode material. Despite these advancements, there is a lack of comprehensive studies on the interaction between stable electrolytes and novel photoanodes. Most research does not conclusively determine whether the poor contact properties between the electrolyte and the photoanode decrease electron mobility in electrolytes. Moreover, few studies explore how the surface morphology of mesoporous semiconductor films affects the contact properties.

A layer of catalytic medium is attached to the other conductive substrate to form a counter electrode in a DSSC. Counter electrodes collect electrons from external circuits, achieving the catalytic reduction in the electrolyte. For the excellent photoelectric performance of DSSCs, the counter electrode is required to possess the characteristics of high electrical conductivity (surface resistance less than 20 Ω/sq), high electrocatalytic activity (charge transition resistance less than 1 Ω/cm2 at the electrode-electrolyte interface), good chemical and mechanical stability, high light reflection ability, and low price, and simple manufacturing process.

Metal Pt is commonly used as a catalytic material of the counter electrode in DSSCs (Atli and Yildiz, 2022). Metal Pt shows high electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic activity, but it is expensive and easy to be oxidized by the REDOX electric pair (iodine radical) in the electrolyte. From the cost perspective, Mehmood and Khan (2019) replaced Pt with lead sulfide as counter electrode material. Under optimal conditions, the PCE of PbS-based DSSC is 4.71%, slightly inferior to the Pt-based devices (5.34%). Jiang et al. (2018) adopted a solvothermal method to synthesize NiSe/NiSe2 hybrid nanomaterials to replace metal Pt as counter electrodes for DSSCs. They delivered an enhanced conversion efficiency of 7.78% for Pt electrodes. The composite Cu–Ni-CoSex quaternary nanocubes are prepared through a one-step selenylation approach (Niu et al., 2019). DSSCs with nanomaterials of Cu–Ni-CoSex display a PCE of 9.74%, superior to that of Pt (8.19%). The outstanding electric catalytic properties of porous nanocube Cu–Ni-CoSex are owing to their low charge transfer resistance and strong synergy of elements. Nanoscale molybdenum nitride had also been used as a counter electrode. However, the electron transport capacity is insufficient to pay for high photoelectric conversion performance (Rajeev et al., 2021). For the counter electrode material Pt, the function improvement of DSSCs is necessary to settle the problem of low stability. Some studies have been conducted to modify Pt electrodes with performance improvement, such as carbon shells with Pt nanoparticle-modified silicon nanowires (Kim et al., 2019, 2020).

The property of counter electrodes relies on the structures and morphologies of materials (Zhou et al., 2017). The porous nanostructure increases the mobility rate of ions. Moreover, most carbon-based materials show regular pores or channels (Li et al., 2014). Carbon nanotubes are good candidates for CE materials in substituting the conventional Pt electrode due to high conductivity, superior chemical stability, excellent catalytic activity, mechanical flexibility, and long-term stability (Shahzad et al., 2022). MWCNTs/N-coordinate Pd2+ complexes (MWCNTs/[PdL2]) are found with a 64.27% relative efficiency (Pt electrode with 100%), as shown in Fig. 11 (a) . The characteristic of the hybrid nanomaterial of DSSCs relies on the functional groups in the molecular structure of Pd2+ complexes and the existence of electron-donating groups (Dayan et al., 2019). Nanomaterials of mesoporous carbon with tellurium (TeMC) have been fabricated as counter electrodes in DSSCs, as exhibited in Fig. 11 (b) (Kim et al., 2020). The nanomaterial of TeMC in the bifacial solar cell exhibits a PCE of 9.43% under front-side irradiation. Nanoflower-like P2Mo18/MoS2@C nanomaterials possess excellent redox performance, high specific surface area, and electrocatalytic activity. PCE reaches 8.85% for P2Mo18/MoS2@C counter electrode-based DSSC (higher than Pt counter electrode-based) due to advantages in structure and component, as demonstrated in Fig. 11 (c) (Li et al., 2019). CoS2/N-doped C@Co-WS2 nanomaterials with the yolk-shell structure are applied as the counter electrode material in DSSCs (Huang et al., 2020). CoS2/NC@Co-WS2 based DSCCs show a superior PCE of 9.21%, superior to that of Pt (8.18%). The yolk-shell structures with coordination of multiple elements are found to possess a large surface area, short charge transport paths, and abundant active sites.

As shown in Figs. 12 (a), 3 D mesoporous rGO with elements of Fe and N as the counter electrode achieves a PCE of 6.52% for a DSSC. A high specific surface area of 654.9 m2/g reduces the mass transfer resistance (Huang et al., 2019). Fig. 12 (b) exemplifies nanomaterials prepared with graphene sheets and gallic acid of Mg2CuSnCoO6. A PCE of 13.30% is achieved when this material is adopted as the counter electrodes of a DSCC. The low band gap material makes a high electron transport rate through the electrode (Oh and Areerob, 2019). Ganesh et al. (2020) reported a low efficiency of 0.72% for 2D Co–Mo–S anchored on graphene nanosheets with nitrogen doping as counter-electrode material in DSSCs. Low efficiency is caused by less conductivity and many defects in the graphene nanosheet. The transformation of flower-like nanostructures with ethylenediamine shows an improvement in efficiency (1.18%) in Fig. 12 (c). In addition, nano-spacers in graphene layers create many spacings and voids. Cationically functionalized graphite with fumed SiO2 shows a similar performance of DSSC with expensive Pt from the perspective of photoelectric performance (Mengal et al., 2018). Unfortunately, a quantitative comparison has not clearly mentioned the costs of functionalized graphite and precious metal Pt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summary of nanomaterials in the counter electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Design and control of nanostructures contribute to the fast electron transport at the counter electrode. As conductors, one-dimensional (1D) metallic materials show significant performance in electrical and optical properties with the action of the quantum confinement effect (Huang and Gao, 2018). Aligned nanoarray forms with one-dimensional nanomaterials are often used as counter electrodes in solar cells. Porous nanomaterial void control requires a strict technological process with the help of a sacrificial agent. Carbon nanomaterials like carbon nanotubes are considered one of the most promising alternatives to precious metals Pt due to the ultra-high conductivity in the axial direction. In addition, the carbon nanomaterial avoids the corrosion issue of metal electrodes under the action of iodine radicals. A carbon matrix modified by a macrocyclic metal has been adopted to replace the Pt counter electrode (Kaur et al., 2021). From an environmental and cost perspective, carbon materials show great promise as future electrode materials. However, the presence of structural defects in carbon materials can lead to a decrease in electrical conductivity. Table 3 summarizes the photoelectric conversion performance of various nanostructured counter electrodes. Notably, the tellurium-doped mesoporous carbon nanomaterial, Cu–Ni-CoSex quaternary porous nanocubes, Pt nanoparticle-decorated silicon nanowire/carbon core-shell nanomaterials, and yolk-shell structured CoS2/N-doped C@Co-WS2 nanomaterials are all potential alternatives to the precious metal Pt, as evidenced by their photoelectric efficiency comparison in Table 3. Although these nanomaterials offer a lower cost compared to metal Pt, it is important to note that the manufacturing inputs required for these nanomaterials or nanostructures are much higher than those for metal Pt. Therefore, metal Pt remains the most suitable choice as electrode material when considering cell efficiency and manufacturing processes. While the present candidates offer potentials for use in counter electrodes, their contributions to cost reduction require further evaluation and consideration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions and remarks about nanomaterials in DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: DSSCs meet the economic and environmental requirements of the third-generation solar industry. A pivotal turning point in enhancing their efficiency transpired in 1992 with the advent of nano-mesoporous semiconductor films (Kresge et al., 1992). Since then, these nano-semiconductor mesoporous films have consistently been the preferred foundation for DSSC photoanodes. Current research on DSSCs focuses on efficiency improvement and industrial applications (high stability), for which the advancement of nanomaterials and nanotechnology emerges as a potent force and a promising solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of component modification on the efficiency of DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The PCE of DSSCs is determined by the electron-hole separation efficiency and electron transport rates, both intricately governed by the performance of the photoanode. From a strategic standpoint, optimizing photoanode is the most important means to improve the photoelectric performance of DSSCs. The spectral response of dyes determines the upper limits of radiation utilization. Although significant progress has been made through molecular structure design, achieving full spectral absorption with a single dye remains infeasible. Quantum dots, as nanoscale semiconductor materials boasting unique photoelectric properties, emerge as an exceptional substitute for conventional dyes. Their advantages extend across a broader spectral absorption range, enhanced photophysical properties, and precise energy level modulation and control. Notably, QDSSCs mark an important stride in breaking the limitations of conventional CSSCs in terms of PCE. Another promising strategy to amplify light absorption within the cell involves co-sensitization. This approach aims to attain strong absorption across the full spectral range by using a complementary array of sensitizers. In the charge transport, electrons traverse the heterogeneous interface between the dye and the semiconductor film. This interface's resistance remarkably influences whether electrons are transferred to the external circuit via the photoanode or prone to recombination within the cell. Elevating the surface modification or morphology control of semiconductor nanomaterials within the photoanode is vital in promoting electron transport. For example, core-shell nanomaterials serve as conduits for electron transport between semiconductor materials and sensitizers, maximizing energy level alignment to ensure a more remarkable electron migration toward the photoanode. Additionally, the design of functional groups on the semiconductor film surface substantially reduces interface transmission resistance, further enhancing the overall charge transport efficiency.

This research proposes a noteworthy correlation between the roles of the electrolyte and the electrode in shaping DSSC efficiency. In the early stage, the inaugural DSSC configuration featured liquid electrolytes paired with Pt electrodes. However, despite subsequent investigations, a sense of dissatisfaction remains due to analogous ionic conductivity in liquid electrolytes and comparable electron conductivity with Pt electrodes. From a holistic perspective centered on cell efficiency, the utilization of liquid electrolyte and Pt electrodes remains an optimal choice for DSSCs, with nanomaterials showcasing minimal impact on improving the properties of these two foundational components.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of component modification on the stability of DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: In case the efficiency standards are fulfilled, stability emerges as the basis for the widespread application of DSSCs. For DSSC applications to be viable, a fundamental prerequisite is long-term stability when subjected to rigorous conditions of intense radiation and elevated temperatures. The key factors imposing limitations on DSSC stability include leakage and volatilization of liquid electrolytes, photocathode corrosion, and sensitizer detachment. Although the development of the solid electrolyte and the quasi-solid electrolyte solves the problems of volatilization and leakage associated with liquid electrolytes, it is at the expense of reduced ion transport and electron transport rates. In addition, temperature fluctuations can transform thermoplastic gel electrolytes from a solid to a solution state, which compromises the stability of DSSCs due to a reduction of viscosity. Introducing nanofillers serves as a conduit for electron transmission, accelerating transport rates and avoiding electron-hole recombination. However, the presence of nanofiller gaps ultimately dampens electron transport rates. The nanofiber-based quasi-solid electrolyte is the most promising alternative to liquid electrolytes, properly addressing PCE and cell stability issues. In addition, the reported nanofiber electrolytes exhibit desirable printability properties, which provides the possibility for subsequent large-scale production of DSSCs. Addressing Pt electrode corrosion necessitates scrutinizing the selection of REDOX pairs and advancing electrode substitutes. In particular, the interaction between the Pt electrode and the REDOX electron pair of I−/I3− can lead to Pt radiolysis into PtI4. Another stability concern arises from DSSCs’ exposure to UV illumination, which accelerates the degradation of the dye molecules (Nakajima and Katoh, 2015). For a 135-day test at a temperature of 20 °C, over 50% of pigments were destroyed in light conditions, while only 30% of pigment destruction was found in the dark (Amogne et al., 2020). Enhancing sensitizer adhesion and stability can be achieved through surface modifications of the semiconductor film and strategic molecular structure design of the dye. Collectively, these efforts converge towards enhancing the stability and reliability of DSSCs, paving the way for their practical and extensive application.

Mozaffari et al. (2017) reviewed emphatically obstacles and restrictions in the fabrication and commercialization of DSSCs, and the performance of DSSCs is inferior to other commercial photovoltaic technology in terms of efficiency, stability, and lifetime. However, many valuable studies have also been conducted to improve the stability of DSSCs. For example, DSSCs have achieved remarkable stability, withstanding 250 h at 60 °C by optimizing the ratio of metal-organic framework and gel electrolytes (Sarwar et al., 2020). The introduction of a copper complex (CuL) and tetradentate ligand as a redox couple resulted in only a 4% decrease in photon-to-current conversion efficiency after 800 h (Yang et al., 2022). The co-sensitization of organic and inorganic dyes has improved stability with 450 h at 27 °C (Teja et al., 2023) and 1000 h at 25 °C (Job et al., 2023). The implementation of an Enger Down Shift of quantum dots has effectively mitigated UV degradation in DSSCs (Riaz et al., 2019). The incorporation of a luminescent down-shifting layer consisting of fluoropolymer coating has enabled DSSCs to maintain stability for over 2000 h (Griffini et al., 2015). In general, physical methods have been adopted to improve the stability of DSSCs, including techniques such as the utilization of sealants, UV filters, and encapsulation.

In this work, an innovative design for DSSCs is conceptualized through a comprehensive analysis of prior research, as illustrated in Fig. 13 . The design involves utilizing intricate three-dimensional dendritic nanostructures to fabricate the mesoporous semiconductor film, in which the primary stem's diameter is restricted to 100 nm to optimize the performance. The dendritic nanostructures ensure rapid electron transport, while their cross-linked branches create an effective barrier, preventing electrolyte penetration into the photoanode. A specialized passivation layer is synthesized for the nanostructures to avoid electron recombination with electrolytes. This layer regulates electron transport by preventing electron spillover. Employing principles of molecular engineering, two metal-free organic dyes are designed to capture the widest possible spectral range. Additionally, quantum dots are strategically used to bridge any spectral gaps through stepwise sensitization. Addressing stability, a printable quasi-solid electrolyte is synthesized using Polymer PVDF-HFP and nanofibers, providing remarkable robustness. These nanofibers span the entire electrolyte layer. Furthermore, a regular array of defection-free single-wall carbon nanotubes is self-grown on the glass substrate, creating a practical pathway for electrolyte permeation. Importantly, all components' energy levels harmoniously align, ensuring seamless electron transport throughout the system.

In the ideal DSSC structure, the hierarchical dendritic nanostructure is first proposed to synthesize the mesoporous semiconductor films. Carbon nanotube arrays are also explored as potential photocathodes. The proposed structure offers a significant improvement over conventional designs by enhancing the coupling effect between various components, which facilitates the reduction of electron and hole transport resistance, thereby boosting efficiency. The performance and stability of these innovative DSSC designs depend critically on the design and synthesis of the nanomaterials used. One of the critical challenges in realizing the ideal structure is the fabrication of the photoanode. Although the regular array of carbon nanotubes can be fabricated using advanced nanotechnology, it is hard to synthesize the dendritic nanostructures with regular morphology. In addition, forming a passivation layer on the dendritic nanostructure presents another technical difficulty due to the surface effects of nanomaterials.

Several factors still impede their commercialization. Due to improvements in the microscopic field, the fabrication process with high-performance requirements for third-generation solar cells is more complicated than for second-generation solar cells. Moreover, the high efficiency observed in small-scale experimental tests must be addressed before large-scale industrialization can be realized. Innovative nanomaterials/nanostructures and micro-scale control techniques offer promising solutions to enhance the PCE of third-generation solar cells. Although nanomaterials are widely reported to manage the characteristics of functional layer and interlayer properties in solar cells, an insightful review of available nanomaterials and their roles in different function regions of solar cells is still lacking. This gap is detrimental to the overall optimal design of nanomaterial-based solar cells. Solar cells are categorized into various functional zones with an insightful review of the strengthening effect and action mechanism of nanomaterials in these zones.

The application of nanomaterials and organic materials contributes to developing the third generation of solar energy. Different from the first (cells based on silicon materials) and second (cells based on thin film) generation solar cells, the third-generation solar cells are characterized by green, efficient (an increase in electricity per unit of peak power), cheap, and long life. Nanomaterials promote the miniaturization, lightweight, and flexibility of third-generation solar cells (Nukunudompanich et al., 2022). Scalable fabrication of third-generation solar cells is still challenging, according to the output and quality. It is required to pay more attention to the long-term performance stability and operating temperature range of third-generation solar cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The challenges of nanomaterials in DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The challenges for DSSCs have been overcome gradually due to the application of nanomaterials and the development of nanotechnology. Nanomaterials optimize the stability and electrode structure of solar cells, which increases the electrochemical reaction of the electrodes and lowers the energy barrier of the electrochemical reaction. Nanomaterials improve the cell capacity, specific power, and life of solar cells by 10%–30%, 25%–35%, and 40%–60%, respectively. The challenges associated with utilizing nanomaterials in DSSCs should also be carefully considered. The main obstacles are reflected in the biosecurity issues and the increasing costs related to the application of these nanomaterials in DSSCs.

Environmental risk The biological effects of nanomaterials have been deeply discussed since 2003 (Service, 2003). Owing to their tiny size, which often falls below the length of an individual cell, the retrieval of nanomaterials proves challenging. The introduction of nanoparticles into the environment will impact both aquatic and soil ecosystems. Nanomaterials enter the environment in production, consumption, and disposal. As migration and conveyance progress, nanomaterials eventually aggregate and settle in water and soil. Upon infiltration into the soil matrix, root systems may absorb nanomaterials and then find passage into plants. Indeed, scientific inquiry attests to the capacity of specific nanoparticles to traverse the rigid cell walls encompassing plant cells. The absorbed nanomaterials may either be transferred to predators or revert to the soil upon plant expiration. It is noteworthy that, when compared to soil, nanomaterials yield a more extensive influence within water bodies due to their rapid transport rate. In aquatic environments, nanoparticles exhibit a spectrum of toxicity mechanisms towards aquatic organisms, mainly including damage to cell integrity, oxidative stress damage, disruption of photosynthetic mechanism, genotoxicity, and release of toxic substances, as shown in Fig. 14 (Wang et al., 2010a, 2010b). These toxicity mechanisms are mainly determined by the properties of nanomaterials.

Health risk In addition to their influence on ecosystem cycles, nanomaterials can even pose potential hazards to human health. Nanoparticles have been associated with adverse effects on respiratory, cardiovascular, immune, and nervous systems. Notably, as early as 2009, prolonged exposure to nanomaterials was linked to the development of pulmonary fibrosis.

Security concerns Nanomaterials exhibit remarkable physical, chemical, and biological properties, which may bring specific safety considerations. The risk of dust explosions requires significant attention for the preparation of large-scale nanomaterials.

Preparation cost The methods employed to synthesize nanomaterials mainly include physical, chemical, and biosynthesis techniques. The nanomaterials possess high purity, uniformity, and controllability with the chemical method, which meets the requirements of electron transport in DSSCs. However, this method requires toxic chemical reagents and harsh reaction conditions, such as high temperature, high pressure, and accurate reaction time. Consequently, the cost of chemical preparation for nanomaterials remains prohibitively high and incompatible with large-scale production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Future research directions for nanomaterials in DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the comprehensive review, it has been well known that the development and application of DSSCs pose significant challenges. It is necessary to give reasonable suggestions for the future development of DSSCs and the application of nanomaterials.

Innovative nanomaterial preparation method Cost-effective and environmentally friendly nanomaterial preparation methods should be developed to accommodate large-scale industrial applications. A pivotal strategy involves judiciously selecting an appropriate activator to improve the preparation conditions, e.g., temperature and pressure.

Nanomaterial recovery To develop a complete process of nanomaterials from recovery to reuse, the process encompasses retrieval, dispersion, extraction, and subsequent reuse of nanomaterials, which is beneficial for cost reduction and environmental protection. The intricate challenges associated with nanometer-scale dimensions have hindered the development of a fully integrated recycling process. In the near future, the recycling of nanomaterials should be considered in the preparation process. Functional attributes, including fluorescence and magnetic properties, should be regarded as rendering nanomaterials amenable to detection and retrieval via external physical fields.

Trade-off between photoelectric efficiency and cost of DSSCs The exploration of trade-offs between photoelectric efficiency and cost in DSSCs is often overlooked in many studies. Understanding the potential balance between enhancing photoelectric efficiency and managing costs is crucial for industrial applications of nanomaterial-based DSSCs. It is important to evaluate in detail the relationship between the extra power gained and the associated increase in input costs, considering the entire manufacturing process rather than only the nanomaterials used. This comprehensive cost-benefit analysis will help clarify the economic viability and scalability of advanced DSSCs in practical applications.

Toxicity evaluation criteria A comprehensive framework for evaluating nanomaterial toxicity should encompass factors such as type, concentration, and size. Employing the response surface method, an elemental influence hierarchy can be initially established. Augmenting this, an artificial neural network (ANN) model grounded in empirical data offers insights into refining the disposal of discarded nanomaterials.

Effective device architectures The enhancement of photoelectric performance in DSSCs is contingent upon the emergence of inventive and efficient device architectures. While current high efficiencies demand intricate manufacturing processes, spanning molecular design, synthesis, and screening of dyes and redox mediators, the pursuit of novel device configurations remains integral.

Substitution of precious metal electrode materials With the mentioned nanomaterials, the electrode is a vital component of DSCCs and perovskite solar cells. It is necessary to investigate how to replace precious metals Pt and Rh with cheap nanomaterials as electrodes. Transition metal nitrides have similar surface structures and chemical properties to the precious metals Pt and Rh, such as high electrical conductivity, remarkable electrochemical stability, and high acid corrosion resistance. One-dimensional carbon nanomaterials possess high electrical conductivity along the axial direction. Developing highly accurate alignment methods is vital for applying one-dimensional nanomaterials in electrode preparation.

Analysis of carrier migration between layers It is necessary to investigate the synergies between material design and interfacial carrier migration characteristics. Interlayer misalignment should be considered in designing the interface structure. One-dimensional nanofiber structures have excellent potential as interlayer electron transport channels, but their regular arrangement and infiltration in functional layers pose significant challenges that must be addressed.

The work focuses on the roles and effects of nanomaterials in DSSCs, and the optimal nanomaterial combination is determined based on the quantitative analysis in each functional area of DSSCs. The ideal structure is a reasonable reference for the nanomaterial selection but not the only valid option. The photoelectric performance of DSSCs is affected by various factors, including materials and components of photoanodes, photocathodes, and electrolytes. It is difficult to determine what factors cause breakthrough improvements in photoelectric performance. In addition, the issues of stability and synthetic costs of nanostructures are not fully considered, which affects the long-term effectiveness and manufacturing costs of DSSCs. An increase in manufacturing costs of nanomaterial-based DSSCs results in a deviation in the positioning of DSSCs in energy systems. For accurate and reliable references of nanomaterial selection, an investigation needs to focus on establishing nanomaterial selection series to meet various requirements. It is necessary to comprehensively test what factor determines the photoelectric performance of DSSCs in research work. For the improvement in the analysis method, most investigations on DSSCs focus on testing parameters of performance evaluation, including PCE, open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current density, and long-time stability. These evaluation parameters intuitively reflect the overall level of the solar cell, and the performance of the internal components of the solar cell is not discussed. Emerging solar cells are rapidly positioned in the market with this testing method, matching their update rate. However, it is insufficient to analyze the components inside the solar cells. It is necessary to develop methods for performance analysis in functional areas of DSSCs. As an integrated system, DSSCs cannot be optimized individually without regard to the support of others. While much attention is placed on improving the performance of single components, this approach often falls short of achieving outstanding PCE due to a lack of synergy. To break through in PCE in future research, more attention should be directed towards the critical issue of energy level matching within the solar cell. This involves considering the synergistic interactions between the REDOX pairs and various components of the solar cell, such as dyes and working electrodes. In addition, the authors believe that incorporating permeable membranes into solar cells could standardize ion transport behavior, necessitating investigations into membranes’ functionality, transparency, and ion selectivity.

Although recent progress has extended the lifespan of third-generation solar cells from a few minutes to hundreds of hours, commercialization remains a distant objective. Moreover, although laboratory efficiency levels have been impressive, their industrial counterparts lag behind. Before specific equipment is successfully introduced to the market, its role in industrial applications needs to be clearly defined. Looking ahead, the emphasis on DSSCs should shift from achieving high PCE to harnessing their inherent advantages of cost-effectiveness and environmental sustainability. Future applications of DSSCs should prioritize adaptability, aligning with the sustainable objectives of solar energy from the customers' perspectives. In the authors’ view, DSSCs offer a compelling option for users due to their lightweight, transparency, and flexibility. They can provide extra electricity for transportation and mobile communication devices with minimal weight increment. In addition, the flexibility of DSSCs offers the possibility for their application in functional textiles. In conclusion, DSSCs will contribute to the diversification of energy systems. DSSCs should be considered as complementary components within the broader landscape of renewable energy systems rather than as primary energy conversion equipment. This shift in perspective underscores their role in a more integrated and sustainable energy future.

Dan Zheng: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xian Yang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Lidija Čuček: Validation, Data curation. Jin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Ting Ma: Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Chungen Yin: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis.
